instruction: What are the most common sideboard mistakes in Legacy?
output: 1) Overboarding: bringing in 8+ cards and cutting core engine pieces. If you cut Brainstorm and Ponder, your deck stops functioning. Maximum 5-6 cards in, 5-6 out. 2) Not knowing what to cut: write sideboard plans BEFORE the tournament. Do not figure it out mid-match. 3) Bringing in hate that hurts your own deck: Null Rod with Lotus Petal, Leyline in a Murktide deck... wait, Leyline only hits opponents. But Grafdigger's Cage stops your own Green Sun's Zenith. 4) Not enough copies of key hate: 1 Leyline is much weaker than 3-4 because you need it in your opening hand. 5) Too narrow: a card that only comes in for 1 matchup is usually worse than a card that comes in for 3.

instruction: What cards should every blue Legacy sideboard include?
output: The universal blue sideboard package: 2-3 Pyroblast (answers Murktide, counters Force, kills blue permanents), 2 Flusterstorm (anti-combo, counter wars), 2-3 Surgical Extraction (any deck can play it at 2 life, targets specific graveyard threats), 1-2 Hydroblast (anti-red in metas with Stompy or Burn). These 7-10 cards form the backbone that every blue sideboard should include. The remaining 5-8 slots are deck-specific: graveyard hate (Leyline), artifact hate (Force of Vigor), sweepers (Toxic Deluge), or matchup-specific cards.

instruction: What is the optimal Force of Will count by archetype?
output: 4 copies: Tempo (Dimir, Delver), Combo (Storm, Show and Tell, Oops, Doomsday) - you NEED to see Force consistently. 3 copies: Control (some builds cut 1 Force for a more proactive card in grindy metas). 2 copies: Very rare, only if your deck has an alternative free interaction plan. 0 copies: Non-blue decks (D&T, Eldrazi, Red Stompy, Burn, Ocelot Pride). There is essentially no reason to play 1 Force - either you need the free interaction (play 4) or you do not (play 0). The 4-or-0 rule is one of Legacy's most consistent deckbuilding principles.

instruction: When should I play Wasteland in my Legacy deck?
output: Play 4 Wasteland when: your deck operates on 1-2 mana (Delver, D&T), you pair it with Daze for mana denial, or your strategy benefits from destroying specific lands (Lands uses it recursively). Play 2-3 Wasteland when: your deck needs some mana denial but also needs colored mana (some midrange builds). Play 0 Wasteland when: you need every land to produce colored mana (3+ color control), your spells cost 3+ mana consistently (you cannot afford to sacrifice a land), or your strategy does not benefit from mana denial (combo decks that need to reach their own mana threshold).

instruction: What makes a good 1-drop threat in Legacy?
output: A Legacy-viable 1-drop creature needs at least one of: 1) Immediate value on ETB (Orcish Bowmasters triggers on opponent's draws). 2) Scaling power (Delver of Secrets flips to 3/2 flyer). 3) Mana generation (Heritage Druid taps 3 elves for GGG). 4) Disruption (Mother of Runes protects other creatures). 5) Evasion (Goblin Lackey puts goblins into play on combat damage). A 1-drop that is just a body (Savannah Lions, 2/1 for W) is not Legacy-playable because the body alone provides no strategic advantage. Every 1-drop slot is precious and must provide utility beyond just attacking.

instruction: How do I decide between maindeck Surgical Extraction and Thoughtseize?
output: Thoughtseize mainboard when: you want proactive disruption against all matchups (takes any nonland card), your deck is tempo or combo (stripping the best card before comboing or deploying threats). Surgical mainboard when: graveyard strategies are prevalent in your meta (15%+ graveyard combo), you want information AND disruption, or your deck is slower and the 2 life from Thoughtseize matters. Most Dimir Tempo plays 4 Thoughtseize main and 2 Surgical side. Some build play 1 Surgical main when graveyard combo exceeds 20% of their local meta. They serve different roles: Thoughtseize is broadest disruption, Surgical is targeted graveyard hate with information.

instruction: How do I build a deck that beats both combo and fair strategies?
output: The best approach is Dimir Tempo's model: 1) Play Force of Will and Daze (handles combo). 2) Play Thoughtseize (handles both by stripping the best card). 3) Play efficient threats that close games quickly (Murktide, Bowmasters). 4) Play Wasteland (handles fair decks' mana bases). 5) Play flexible removal (Snuff Out is free vs creatures). The key insight: you do not need dedicated combo hate or fair-deck hate. You need DISRUPTION that works against everything, plus a fast clock. Force of Will is the universal answer to combo. Wasteland + Daze is the universal answer to fair decks. Thoughtseize works against both. This is why Dimir Tempo is the most played deck.

instruction: What is the role of each card in the Dimir Tempo core?
output: 4 Brainstorm: finds answers, sets up Delver flips, hides cards from Thoughtseize. 4 Ponder: additional card selection, shuffle away bad cards. 4 Force of Will: free countermagic, protects threats, stops combo. 4 Daze: free counter on turns 1-3, forces opponents to play off-curve. 4 Thoughtseize: proactive disruption, sees opponent's hand, strips best card. 4 Wasteland: mana denial, pairs with Daze, removes utility lands. 4 Orcish Bowmasters: punishes cantrips, flash threat, creates tokens. 3-4 Murktide Regent: primary finisher, large flyer, dodges Bolt. Every card serves a specific role in the tempo strategy. Removing any one of these 8 card types significantly weakens the deck.

instruction: Why does Reanimator play Entomb?
output: Entomb puts any card from your library into your graveyard for one black mana at instant speed. In Reanimator, this is a tutor for your reanimation targets: Atraxa, Griselbrand, Archon of Cruelty, or Emrakul. You can Entomb on your opponent's end step and Reanimate on your turn, or combo on turn 1 with Dark Ritual into Entomb plus Reanimate. No other card finds reanimation targets this efficiently.

instruction: Why does 8-Cast play Thought Monitor?
output: Thought Monitor has affinity for artifacts, meaning it costs less for each artifact you control. In 8-Cast, which floods the board with cheap artifacts like Mox Opal, Mishra's Bauble, and artifact lands, Thought Monitor often costs just UU or even U for a 2/2 flyer that draws two cards. This card advantage keeps the engine running and is one of the best payoffs for artifact density.

instruction: Why does Lands play Exploration?
output: Exploration lets you play an additional land each turn for just one green mana. In Lands, this means deploying two utility lands per turn: double Wasteland, Wasteland plus Rishadan Port, or accelerating to Dark Depths combo. Combined with Life from the Loam returning three lands per turn, Exploration ensures you never run out of land drops while denying opponents their mana.

instruction: Why does Dimir Tempo play 4 Wasteland despite being a two-color deck?
output: Wasteland is essential to Dimir Tempo's mana denial strategy. Combined with Daze (bouncing your own land), Wasteland creates situations where the opponent cannot cast spells. Destroying a dual land after the opponent bounced their other land to Daze back creates complete mana denial. The colorless mana is manageable because the deck runs cheap spells and cantrips to find colored sources.

instruction: Why does Death and Taxes play Rishadan Port?
output: Rishadan Port taps target land during the opponent's upkeep, denying them a mana source for that turn. Combined with Wasteland and Thalia's tax effect, D&T creates a comprehensive mana denial package. Porting a dual land when the opponent needs two colors is devastating. It does not sacrifice itself like Wasteland, so the mana denial is repeatable every turn. This soft lock gives D&T time to assemble its creature synergies.

instruction: Why does Storm play Burning Wish?
output: Burning Wish retrieves a sorcery from your sideboard, giving Storm access to a toolbox of win conditions and utility. It finds Tendrils of Agony to win, Past in Flames to rebuild, Empty the Warrens for grindy games, and more. Burning Wish effectively turns your sideboard into an extension of your deck. It also works with Lion's Eye Diamond since you crack LED with Wish on the stack.

instruction: Why do most Legacy decks play 4 fetchlands minimum?
output: Fetchlands provide three critical functions in Legacy: they fix mana by finding dual lands, they shuffle away unwanted cards put back by Brainstorm and Ponder, and they fill the graveyard for delve (Murktide Regent) and threshold mechanics. The 1 life cost is negligible. A deck with 4 Brainstorm needs fetchlands to function - without them, Brainstorm is just a delayed card draw.

instruction: Why does Eldrazi Stompy play Chalice of the Void?
output: Chalice on 1 counters almost every cantrip in Legacy (Brainstorm, Ponder, Swords to Plowshares), effectively locking out blue decks. Eldrazi Stompy can deploy Chalice on turn 1 via Ancient Tomb or City of Traitors because its own threats cost 2-5 mana and are unaffected. This proactive disruption buys time for Thought-Knot Seer and Reality Smasher to close the game before opponents recover.

instruction: Why does Goblins play Goblin Matron?
output: Goblin Matron tutors for any Goblin card when she enters the battlefield. This toolbox ability lets Goblins find exactly what it needs: Goblin Ringleader for card advantage, Munitions Expert for removal, Goblin Trashmaster for artifact hate, or Sling-Gang Lieutenant for a sacrifice combo finish. Via Aether Vial, Matron enters at instant speed, providing a surprise tutor on the opponent's turn.

instruction: Why does Painter play Red Elemental Blast and Pyroblast as an 8-pack?
output: With Painter's Servant naming blue, every permanent and spell in the game is blue. Red Elemental Blast and Pyroblast each become unconditional counterspells and unconditional removal for 1 mana. Running all 8 copies provides consistency in finding these universal answers. Even without Painter active, they are strong sideboard cards in a blue-dominated format.

instruction: Why does Infect play Invigorate?
output: Invigorate gives +4/+4 for free if the opponent controls a Forest (which many Legacy decks have via fetchable duals). Combined with Glistener Elf's infect, a free +4/+4 represents 5 poison counters. Turn 2 kills are possible with Glistener Elf plus Invigorate plus another pump spell. The opponent gaining 3 life is irrelevant because Infect wins via poison counters, not damage.

instruction: Why does Doomsday play Thassa's Oracle?
output: Thassa's Oracle wins the game when it enters the battlefield if your devotion to blue is equal to or greater than the number of cards in your library. After casting Doomsday (which reduces your library to 5 cards), you construct a pile that draws through those 5 cards and lands Oracle with an empty library. This is the cleanest Doomsday win condition since it cannot be stopped by creature removal once it resolves.

instruction: Why do some Dimir Tempo lists play Psychic Frog over Delver of Secrets?
output: Psychic Frog provides a more consistent threat than Delver. Delver requires blind flips or cantrip setup and stays a 1/1 until it flips. Frog grows by discarding cards (which fills the graveyard for Murktide), gains evasion on demand, and can exile cards from graveyards for value. It also dodges Orcish Bowmasters ping damage by growing in response. The tradeoff is 2 mana versus 1.

instruction: Why does Sneak and Show play Omniscience alongside Sneak Attack?
output: Omniscience provides a different angle of attack than Sneak Attack. While Sneak gives creatures haste but sacrifices them, Omniscience lets you cast anything for free permanently. Against decks with Karakas (bouncing legendary Emrakul) or instant-speed removal, Omniscience is better because you can cast multiple threats and protection. Having both ensures redundancy in the combo package.

instruction: Why does Jeskai Control play Up the Beanstalk?
output: Up the Beanstalk draws a card when it enters and draws another whenever you cast a spell with mana value 5 or more. In Jeskai Control, pitch spells like Force of Will, Solitude, and Fury have mana values of 5 and 3+ respectively. Force of Will cast via alternate cost still has mana value 5, triggering Beanstalk. This turns defensive plays into card advantage, keeping the control engine fueled.

instruction: Why does Lands play Punishing Fire?
output: Punishing Fire deals 2 damage for 1R and returns to your hand whenever an opponent gains life. Paired with Grove of the Burnwillows (which gives opponents 1 life for red or green mana), you create an infinite loop of 2-damage removal. This repeatable removal engine clears small creatures every turn. Against creature decks, Punishing Fire plus Grove is a soft lock.

instruction: Why does Dredge play Lion's Eye Diamond?
output: Lion's Eye Diamond sacrifices to add 3 mana of any color while discarding your hand. In Dredge, discarding your hand is actually beneficial because it puts dredge cards like Golgari Grave-Troll and Stinkweed Imp into the graveyard where they can be dredged. The 3 mana helps cast Breakthrough or activate Cephalid Coliseum. LED turns a drawback into an advantage in this archetype.

instruction: Why does Death and Taxes play exactly 1 Batterskull and 1 Kaldra Compleat?
output: Stoneforge Mystic tutors equipment, so you only need 1 copy of each. Running 1 Batterskull and 1 Kaldra gives you options: Batterskull for lifelink and grindy matchups, Kaldra for hasty indestructible pressure. Drawing a second copy of either equipment is terrible since they are expensive to hardcast. The singleton approach maximizes Stoneforge's flexibility while minimizing dead draws.

instruction: Why does Izzet Delver play Dragon's Rage Channeler?
output: Dragon's Rage Channeler is a 1-mana threat that surveys (look at top, keep or bin) to fuel delirium and grows to a 3/3 flyer with delirium active. In Izzet Delver with Mishra's Bauble, fetchlands, cantrips, and Lightning Bolt, delirium activates by turn 2-3 consistently. DRC provides a proactive threat that also fills your graveyard for Murktide Regent's delve cost. It does double duty as threat and enabler.

instruction: Why does Bant Control play Swords to Plowshares over other removal?
output: Swords to Plowshares exiles for 1 mana at instant speed with no restrictions on creature size. In Legacy, exile is crucial to handle Uro (escapes from graveyard), Murktide (massive flyer), and reanimation targets. The life gain for the opponent is irrelevant when you are playing a long game. No other removal spell in the format offers this combination of efficiency, unconditional targeting, and exile.

instruction: Why do some Legacy sideboards include Surgical Extraction?
output: Surgical Extraction costs 0 mana (2 life via phyrexian mana) and exiles all copies of a card from graveyard, hand, and library. It is devastating against combo decks that rely on specific cards: Reanimate targets, Doomsday piles, Dark Depths, and Show and Tell targets. The information from seeing the opponent's hand and library is also valuable. It is one of the most flexible anti-combo sideboard tools.

instruction: Why does Enchantress play Argothian Enchantress and Sythis?
output: Both are enchantress effects that draw cards when you cast enchantments. Argothian Enchantress has shroud making it hard to remove, while Sythis also gains 1 life per enchantment. Running both maximizes the chance of having a draw engine online. With 20+ enchantments in the deck, each enchantress draws 3-5 extra cards per game. This card advantage engine is what makes the deck function.

instruction: Why does Mono-Red Prison play Chrome Mox?
output: Chrome Mox provides turn-1 acceleration by exiling a nonartifact, nonland colored card. Combined with Ancient Tomb and City of Traitors, Red Prison can deploy turn-1 Blood Moon, Trinisphere, or Chalice of the Void. The card disadvantage of exiling a card is acceptable when your lock piece prevents the opponent from playing the game. Speed is everything in prison strategies.

instruction: Why does Cradle Control play Gaea's Cradle?
output: Gaea's Cradle taps for green mana equal to the number of creatures you control. With even 3 creatures, it produces more mana than any other land in the format. Cradle Control uses Green Sun's Zenith to find creatures and build a board, then leverages Cradle mana for enormous plays like hardcasting Craterhoof Behemoth or casting multiple spells in one turn. It is the engine that powers the deck.

instruction: Why does Death and Taxes play Mother of Runes?
output: Mother of Runes gives a target creature protection from a chosen color for one turn. This protects Thalia from Lightning Bolt, makes Stoneforge Mystic immune to Swords to Plowshares, and provides unblockable attacks by giving protection from the blocker's color. At 1 mana, it comes down early and demands an immediate answer. If unanswered, it makes every subsequent creature threat nearly impossible to deal with.

instruction: Why does Legacy Storm play Defense Grid in the sideboard?
output: Defense Grid makes spells cast on a non-active player's turn cost 3 more. When it is your turn and you are comboing off, your opponent's Force of Will and Flusterstorm each cost 3 additional mana. This effectively shuts off free countermagic. Storm brings in Defense Grid against blue decks to protect its combo turn. At 2 mana, it can be deployed the turn before you go off.

instruction: Why does Bant Miracles play Terminus?
output: Terminus puts all creatures on the bottom of their owners' libraries for just 1 white mana if it is the first card drawn this turn (miracle cost). With Brainstorm and Ponder to set up the top of your library, miracling Terminus is consistent. Putting creatures on the bottom is better than destroying them because it avoids death triggers, indestructible, and graveyard recursion. It is the most efficient board wipe in Legacy.

instruction: Why do Legacy decks typically run 18-20 lands instead of 24?
output: Legacy's mana curve is extremely low, with most impactful spells costing 1-2 mana. Additionally, 8 cantrips (4 Brainstorm + 4 Ponder) help find lands when needed. Running fewer lands means more spell density and fewer dead draws. Tempo decks run 18-19, control decks run 20-22, and combo decks vary from 14-20 depending on mana requirements. The cantrip shell compensates for lower land counts.

instruction: Why does Oops All Spells play no lands?
output: Oops All Spells uses Balustrade Spy or Undercity Informer, which mills until you hit a land. With zero lands in the deck, this mills your entire library. Then Narcomoeba triggers, Bridge from Below creates tokens, and Dread Return flashbacks targeting Thassa's Oracle with an empty library wins the game. The no-land design is the entire combo engine. Mana comes from Spirit Guides and Lotus Petal.

instruction: Why does Cephalid Breakfast play Nomads en-Kor?
output: Nomads en-Kor can redirect damage to another creature for 0 mana, unlimited times. With Cephalid Illusionist in play (which mills three cards whenever it becomes the target of a spell or ability), you target Illusionist with Nomads' ability repeatedly for free, milling your entire library. Then you win with Thassa's Oracle or Narcomoeba plus Dread Return. Nomads provides the free, repeatable targeting.

instruction: Why does Red Stompy play Goblin Rabblemaster?
output: Goblin Rabblemaster creates a 1/1 Goblin token each combat that must attack. Rabblemaster itself gets +1/+0 for each attacking Goblin. Behind a Chalice on 1 or Blood Moon, the opponent often cannot remove Rabblemaster, and it snowballs quickly: turn 1 lock piece, turn 2 Rabblemaster, turn 3 attack with Rabblemaster as a 4/2 plus tokens. It is an efficient clock that punishes slow starts.

instruction: Why does Aluren play Acererak the Archlich?
output: Acererak is the combo piece that wins with Aluren. With Aluren in play, you cast Acererak for free (mana value 3 or less). When it enters, you venture into a dungeon. If you choose any dungeon except the final room of Lost Mine of Phandelver, Acererak bounces to your hand. You then recast it for free via Aluren, venturing again. This infinite loop drains the opponent through dungeon effects.

instruction: Why does Maverick play Knight of the Reliquary?
output: Knight of the Reliquary sacrifices a Forest or Plains to search for any land. In Maverick, this provides a land toolbox: find Wasteland for mana denial, Karakas for legendary removal, Maze of Ith for defense, or Dryad Arbor for an extra creature. Knight grows as lands enter the graveyard, often becoming a 6/6 or larger. Combined with fetchlands naturally filling the graveyard, Knight is both a tutor and a threat.

instruction: Why does Dimir Tempo play 4 Force of Will even though pitching a blue card is costly?
output: Force of Will is mandatory for surviving combo matchups. Without Force, you lose to turn-1 Reanimator, Oops All Spells, and fast Storm kills before you can deploy threats. The card disadvantage of pitching a blue card is offset by the tempo gain of countering a game-winning spell for free. Dimir Tempo runs enough blue cards (12-16) to fuel Force consistently. Not playing Force in a blue deck is almost never correct.

instruction: Why does Elves play Glimpse of Nature?
output: Glimpse of Nature draws a card whenever you cast a creature spell that turn. In Elves, with Heritage Druid, Nettle Sentinel, and cheap elves, you can chain 5-10 creatures in a single turn, each drawing a card. This turns a small board into a massive card draw engine that finds Craterhoof Behemoth or Natural Order for the win. Glimpse is the most explosive engine in the Elves deck.

instruction: Why does Stoneblade play 2 copies of Counterspell?
output: Counterspell is an unconditional hard counter for UU that hits any spell type. In Stoneblade, which plays a longer game than Delver, having hard counters for anything is valuable. Unlike Daze (which falls off late game) or Spell Pierce (conditional), Counterspell reliably stops Show and Tell, planeswalkers, and combo pieces at any stage. Two copies provides consistency without overcrowding the two-mana slot.

instruction: Why does Legacy Burn play Eidolon of the Great Revel?
output: Eidolon deals 2 damage to any player who casts a spell with mana value 3 or less. In Legacy, the vast majority of spells cost 1-2 mana. Each Brainstorm, Force of Will (alternate cost still has mana value 5, so it does NOT trigger Eidolon), and Swords to Plowshares the opponent casts deals them 2. Combined with Price of Progress against nonbasic-heavy manabases, Eidolon creates a damage clock just by existing.

instruction: Why does Jeskai Control play Prismatic Ending over other removal?
output: Prismatic Ending exiles nonland permanents with mana value X or less, where X is the number of colors of mana spent. In a three-color deck, it exiles anything with mana value 3 or less, covering most Legacy threats. Exile is superior to destroy in a format with recursive threats. It also hits noncreature permanents like Sylvan Library and Aether Vial, making it more versatile than Swords to Plowshares.

instruction: Why do Legacy combo decks often play exactly 14-15 lands?
output: Legacy combo decks need fewer lands because they plan to win on turns 1-3 and supplement mana with acceleration like Lotus Petal, Dark Ritual, Chrome Mox, and Lion's Eye Diamond. More lands mean fewer combo pieces and less consistency. With cantrips to find the 1-2 lands needed, 14-15 is the sweet spot that minimizes flood while avoiding complete mana screw. Every non-land card matters in combo.

instruction: Why does Merfolk play Aether Vial over other acceleration?
output: Aether Vial lets Merfolk deploy creatures at instant speed without paying mana, bypassing Chalice of the Void and countermagic. This frees up mana for Force of Will and activated abilities. Vial on 2 puts in lords (Lord of Atlantis, Master of the Pearl Trident) at instant speed for surprise lethal damage in combat. No other accelerant provides both free deployment and instant-speed surprise factor.

instruction: Why does Death and Taxes sometimes splash red for Magus of the Moon?
output: Red splash via Plateau and Aether Vial gives D&T access to Magus of the Moon, which turns all nonbasic lands into Mountains. D&T runs many basics and Plains, so it is less affected than opponents. Magus via Vial at instant speed can lock opponents out of colors. The red splash also provides access to Pyroblast effects in the sideboard for blue matchups. This version is called Imperial Taxes.

instruction: Why does Nic Fit play Veteran Explorer?
output: Veteran Explorer is a 1/1 for G that, when it dies, lets each player search for two basic lands. Nic Fit pairs it with Cabal Therapy (sacrifice Explorer as the flashback cost) to ramp to 4+ mana while disrupting the opponent's hand. Most Legacy decks run 1-3 basics, so you gain more from the ramp than they do. This acceleration lets Nic Fit cast Primeval Titan, planeswalkers, and other expensive cards ahead of schedule.

instruction: Why do some Legacy decks play Mishra's Bauble alongside Urza's Saga?
output: Mishra's Bauble is a 0-cost artifact that Urza's Saga's chapter III can tutor for. It adds artifact count for Saga's Construct tokens, enables delirium by providing an artifact type in the graveyard, and replaces itself with a delayed draw. The Saga-Bauble package creates consistency and synergy: Saga finds Bauble when you need a free cantrip, and Bauble makes Saga's tokens larger.

instruction: Why does Lands play The Tabernacle at Pendrell Vale?
output: Tabernacle forces all creatures to pay 1 mana during their controller's upkeep or be destroyed. Lands plays few or no creatures, so it is largely unaffected. Against creature decks, Tabernacle is devastating: paying upkeep for 3-4 creatures each turn is impossible while also deploying new threats. Combined with Wasteland and Rishadan Port denying mana, Tabernacle creates an inescapable lock on creature strategies.

instruction: Why does Sneak and Show play Atraxa Grand Unifier alongside Emrakul?
output: Atraxa provides a different angle than Emrakul. When put into play via Show and Tell, Atraxa's ETB draws up to 7 cards (one from each card type), immediately refilling your hand with countermagic and more threats. Emrakul has annihilator 6 but no ETB from Show and Tell. Against removal-heavy decks, Atraxa's card advantage matters more than Emrakul's raw power since drawing 5+ cards is often game-winning.

instruction: Why does Storm play Ponder and Brainstorm but not Preordain?
output: Ponder sees three cards deep and can shuffle, while Brainstorm digs three deep and pairs with fetches. Both provide maximum card selection for finding combo pieces. Preordain only looks at two cards and is less powerful in a deck that needs to assemble specific combinations. Storm needs density of cantrips that see the most cards per mana spent. Four Ponder plus four Brainstorm is the standard, with no room for Preordain.

instruction: Why does Dimir Tempo play only 2 Underground Sea instead of 4?
output: Running 2 Underground Sea reduces vulnerability to Wasteland. If your opponent destroys your only dual land, having basics as backup keeps you in the game. Dimir Tempo supplements with basic Island, basic Swamp, and fetchlands. The deck's spells cost only U or B, so a single Underground Sea plus basics is sufficient for color requirements. Two copies ensures you see one via fetches without overexposing to land destruction.

instruction: Why does Dredge play Breakthrough?
output: Breakthrough for X=0 draws 4 cards and discards your hand. In Dredge, discarding dredge cards is the goal: Golgari Grave-Troll, Stinkweed Imp, and Shambling Shell all go to the graveyard where they can be dredged. The 4 cards drawn can each be replaced with dredge on future turns. Breakthrough is the fastest way to fill the graveyard alongside Lion's Eye Diamond and Careful Study.

instruction: Why does 8-Cast play Emry Lurker of the Loch?
output: Emry costs less for each artifact you control and can tap to cast artifact spells from your graveyard. In 8-Cast, she often costs just U and provides recursion for destroyed artifacts, Mishra's Baubles for repeated draws, and milled Thought Monitors. She also fills the graveyard via her ETB mill 4, fueling further synergies. Emry provides both a body and a recursion engine for one mana.

instruction: Why does Legacy Dimir Tempo run basic Island and basic Swamp?
output: Basic lands are immune to Wasteland and Blood Moon, providing guaranteed colored mana. In the mirror match and against other Wasteland decks, having basics means you always have access to your colors. One basic Island and one basic Swamp ensure you can fetch either color safely. This is essential insurance in a format where mana denial is a primary strategy.

instruction: Why does Painter play Imperial Recruiter?
output: Imperial Recruiter tutors for any creature with power 2 or less when it enters. In Painter, this finds Painter's Servant (1/3), Goblin Engineer (1/3), and various utility creatures. It provides consistency in assembling the Painter-Grindstone combo. Combined with Goblin Welder for recursion and Imperial Recruiter chains (Recruiter finds Recruiter), the deck never lacks for combo pieces.
